coredata.dc:title: Interaction of toxic metals with penta variant functional groups decorated carbon quantum dots: An in-silico investigation using density functional theory calculations towards water remediation/quality monitoring applications

coredata.dc:description: Using quantum chemical density functional theory, we have examined the detection ability of functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different sizes and five functional groups (carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), amino (NH2), thiol (S-H), and phosphono (H2PO3)) towards the toxic metals iron (Fe), chromium (Cr), selenium (Se), mercury (Hg), antimony (Sb), and cadmium (Cd) in both gas and solvent (water) phases. Preliminary screening through interaction studies has projected that the NH2-functionalized and COOH-functionalized CQDs had greater adsorption affinities toward the toxic metals. Further, the interaction of toxic metals with functionalized CQDs is qualitatively assessed through optical and magnetic transduction phenomena. The highest percentage of decrement in ultraviolet-visible absorption peaks is observed for Fe with COOH-functionalized CQD-C32H15O2 (69.91 %), Se with OH-functionalized CQD-C31O15H15 (44.56 %), Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD-C31H16N (98.42 %), Cr SH-functionalized CQD -C19H11S (39.16 %), Fe with H2PO3-functionalized CQD-C31H16O3P (71.48 %). Furthermore, the appearance of new characteristic peaks in Raman spectra has confirmed the toxic metal complexation ability of the functionalized CQDs. Interestingly, the highest lifetime is observed in Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD C31H16N (31.52 ns). Finally, we can observe an increase in magnetic moment upon metal complexation with the functionalized CQDs. In summary, our investigations have proved that functionalized CQDs can act as adsorbents toward Fe, Cr, and Se while they can detect Hg and Sb in the drinking water making them as suitable candidates for water remediation as well as quality monitoring applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using quantum chemical density functional theory, we have examined the detection ability of functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different sizes and five functional groups (carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), amino (NH2), thiol (S-H), and phosphono (H2PO3)) towards the toxic metals iron (Fe), chromium (Cr), selenium (Se), mercury (Hg), antimony (Sb), and cadmium (Cd) in both gas and solvent (water) phases. Preliminary screening through interaction studies has projected that the NH2-functionalized and COOH-functionalized CQDs had greater adsorption affinities toward the toxic metals. Further, the interaction of toxic metals with functionalized CQDs is qualitatively assessed through optical and magnetic transduction phenomena. The highest percentage of decrement in ultraviolet-visible absorption peaks is observed for Fe with COOH-functionalized CQD-C32H15O2 (69.91 %), Se with OH-functionalized CQD-C31O15H15 (44.56 %), Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD-C31H16N (98.42 %), Cr SH-functionalized CQD -C19H11S (39.16 %), Fe with H2PO3-functionalized CQD-C31H16O3P (71.48 %). Furthermore, the appearance of new characteristic peaks in Raman spectra has confirmed the toxic metal complexation ability of the functionalized CQDs. Interestingly, the highest lifetime is observed in Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD C31H16N (31.52 ns). Finally, we can observe an increase in magnetic moment upon metal complexation with the functionalized CQDs. In summary, our investigations have proved that functionalized CQDs can act as adsorbents toward Fe, Cr, and Se while they can detect Hg and Sb in the drinking water making them as suitable candidates for water remediation as well as quality monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Access to safe and drinkable water is crucial for a strong and healthy society. Nearly 10 % of people worldwide, according to estimates from the World Health Organisation (WHO), lack access to clean drinking water sources [1]. Water is considered safe when it is devoid of biological pathogens, physical waste and pollutants, chemical toxins, carcinogens, radionuclides, or any other substances that pose a health risk. Human exposure to contaminated and unsanitary water leads to the transmission of various infectious diseases, such as cholera, diarrhea, hepatitis A, and typhoid fever [2]. According to statistics provided by the WHO, around 8,29,000 people die a year due to the consumption of contaminated water. Out of these deaths, 2420 are caused by cholera in 38 different countries, 2,00,000 are ascribed to typhoid fever, and 7134 occur as a result of hepatitis A [2]. The contamination of water occurs due to increased anthropogenic sources on the release of toxic metals into the water medium without proper treatment. It is estimated that approximately one million individuals succumb to waterborne maladies annually. Furthermore, it is estimated that 1.5 million children will perish from diarrhea alone, while approximately 37.7 million are affected by waterborne diseases [3]. Metals, such as chromium (Cr), iron (Fe), selenium (Se), cadmium (Cd), antimony (Sb), and mercury, are potentially highly toxic and hazardous to human health. Chromium (Cr) contamination of water arises from electroplating, tannery, dyeing, and mining industries [4,5]. The most common oxidation states of Cr ions in water are + 3 and + 6. Long-term exposure to Cr through water beyond the permissible limit leads to stomach cancer, carcinogenesis, skin ulcerations, and asthma [4]. The maximum permissible limit of Cr ions in drinking water set by the WHO is 0.05 mg/L[5]. Iron (Fe) is one of the most abundant elements on earth and it serves as a vital mineral nutrient, which plays a role in the maintenance of energy metabolism. It is a critical component of hemoglobin and myoglobin, as well as numerous enzymes [6]. Excessive accumulation of Fe in water arises from steel, metallurgical, and mining industries [6]. The most common oxidation states of Fe ions in water are + 2 and + 3 [7]. Continued exposure to Fe through water leads to hemochromatosis, retinitis, choroiditis, cancer, and heart disorders [6]. The maximum permissible limit of Fe ions in drinking water set by the WHO is 0.3 mg/L [6]. In trace amounts, selenium (Se) serves as a crucial and necessary nutrient, so it is beneficial for human health. Elevated levels of Se concentration in water occur due to metal smelting, mining, coal-fired plants, oil refineries, and agricultural drainage [8]. The most common oxidation states of Se ions in water are + 4 and + 6. Prolonged exposure to Se via water causes hair loss, nail loss, skin irritation, eye irritation, gastrointestinal abnormalities, and neurological disorders [8]. The maximum permissible limit of Se ions in drinking water set by the USEPA (United States Environmental Protection Agency) is 0.05 mg/L. Cadmium (Cd) contamination of water arises from corrosion of galvanized pipe, erosion of natural deposits, discharge from metal refineries, and runoff from waste batteries and paint products [9]. Prolonged exposure to Cd via water causes nephropathy, atherosclerosis, renal anemia, low bone mineral density, and osteoporosis [9,10]. The maximum permissible limit of Cd ions in drinking water set by the WHO is 0.003 mg/L [11]. Antimony (Sb) contamination of water arises from mining, smelting, polyethylene terephthalate plastic production, lead acid batteries, and pump sealants industries [12]. The most common oxidation states of Sb ions in water are + 3 and + 5. Chronic exposure to Sb through water causes pneumoconiosis, atherosclerosis, renal anemia, low bone mineral density, osteoporosis, cardiovascular disorders, and gastrointestinal effects [13]. The maximum admissible antimony level in drinking water set by the WHO is 0.005 mg/L [14]. Mercury (Hg) contamination of water arises from solid waste incineration (medical waste), coal combustion, oil combustion, pyrometallurgical processes, mining activities, and the production of gold [15,16] . The most common oxidation states of Hg ions in water are + 1 and + 2. Prolonged exposure to Hg via water causes idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy, impaired hemoglobin formation, renal cancer, and nephrotic syndrome, and inhibits the production of the digestive trypsin, chymotrypsin [17]. The maximum admissible Hg level in drinking water set by the WHO is 0.001 mg/L [18].

Functionalized CQDs have been employed in a wide range of applications such as chemical sensing[19], biosensing[20], drug delivery[21], photocatalysis[22], and electrocatalysis[23]. Functionalization of CQDs enhances the electronic, and optical properties such as fluorescence, absorbance, bio-compatibility, more active sites, large surface area, and solubility of CQDs in aqueous solvents thus leading to improvement in the performance of CQDs as sensors and adsorbents [24–26]. Hydroxyl group functionalized CQDs were used for the detection of toxic metals such as Hg2 +, Fe3+, and Cr6+ in a water medium using an optical sensing method [27,28]. Detection of Cr6+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ ions employing carboxyl group functionalized CQDs has been investigated by Lua et al., Latief et al., and Ye et al., respectively using a quenching mechanism [29–31]. The detection of Cr6+ ions using amino group functionalized CQD by quenching mechanism has been reported [28,32]. Studies on detecting Cr6+ in aqueous solution using amine-functionalized CQDs via the quenching mechanism have been reported [33] . The detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions using L-cysteine functionalized carbon dot by quenching mechanism has been investigated [34] . The detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions using phosphate-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots based on the quenching mechanism has been reported [35] . Carboxyl-functionalized CQD and amino- functionalized CQD were used as an adsorbent for the removal of Cd has been reported [36]. Barium hydroxide-functionalized graphene QD has been used as a nanobiosorbent for the removal of lead and lanthanum ions has been reported [37]. Poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate-functionalized carbon dots was used as an adsorbent for the removal of Fe3+ ions has been reported [38]. Graphene oxide rich in hydroxyl functional group has been used as an adsorbent for the removal of Cd was reported [39].

Potassium-doped graphene oxide was used for the determination of Fe3+ ions in aqueous phase by adsorption process through DFT was reported [40]. Carboxymethyl cellulose metal-organic framework (MOF) and graphene were used for the determination of Cr metal ion in water phase by adsorption process through DFT were reported [41,42]. Determination of Se4+ and Se6+ ions using UiO-66, UiO-66-NH2 MOF by adsorption process through DFT was reported [43]. Determination of mercury in the water phase using graphene by adsorption process through DFT was reported [42].Carbon nanofibers decorated Zirconium oxide was used for the determination of Sb3+ and Sb5+ ions in the water phase by adsorption process through DFT was reported [44]. Boron nanocluster and graphene was used for the determination of cadmium in aqueous phase by adsorption process through DFT was reported [42,45].

There are only a few reports on functionalized CQDs. In this work, we have examined the potential of several functionalized rectangular CQDs of different sizes and functional groups (carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), amino (NH2), thiol (S-H), and phosphono (H2PO3)) to detect hazardous metals like Fe, Cr, Hg, Se, Sb, and Cd using quantum chemical density functional theory (DFT). We have carried out the structural assessment through formation energy analysis. The electronic properties are understood through the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and energy gap analyses. More importantly, the optical properties are explored through UV-Vis and Raman spectra analyses. The adsorption behavior of functionalized CQDs towards Fe, Cr, Hg, Se, Sb, and Cd in both gas and water phases is examined. The fluorescence properties of the developed functionalized CQDs have been studied in the aqueous phase before and after the interaction of the hazardous metals with them to validate their optical sensing capabilities. This paper represents the first known attempt to computationally evaluate the detection of harmful elements using functionalized CQDs of different sizes and functional groups using their optical sensing properties. Also, we have evaluated the magnetic properties of the functionalized CQDs before and after metal adsorption to exhibit them as a magnetic sensor. Overall, this extensive DFT study has proved the dual sensing ability of the functionalized CQDs toward toxic metals found in potable water. This research will be a step towards developing quantum optical sensors for water remediation and quality monitoring applications.

All calculations were carried out using the quantum chemical DFT package Gaussian16 with B3LYP exchange-correlation functional and 6–311 G* *(d,p)/LANL2DZ basis sets [46]. B3LYP functional has been chosen due to high accuracy and it agrees well with experimental observations [47,48].For elements such as carbon (C), hydrogen(H), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), sulphur (S), and phosphorus (P), the usage of 6–311 G* *(d,p) basis set has been recommended based on previous theoretical studies [49]. Functionalized CQDs were modeled and optimized to their ground state. The details of the modeled systems are given in Table S1. All the systems were passivated with hydrogen (H) atoms to ensure their stability. The functionalized CQDs were designed by replacing the H with the COOH, OH, NH2, SH, and phosphono (H2PO3) functional groups (Table S1). The details on all functionalized CQDs are provided in the Supplementary Information (SI). The following formula was used to find the formation energy of a functionalized CQD system [50]:

In addition, the study investigated the interaction between functionalized CQDs and toxic elements such as Fe, Cr, Hg, Se, Sb, and Cd. The calculation of the interaction energy between metals and functionalized CQDs was determined using the formula:

UV-Vis absorption and Raman spectra of the pure and metal-adsorbed functionalized CQDs were obtained by performing time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations as reported in our earlier work [43].

The optimized structures of the functionalized CQDs are shown in Fig. 1. The C-H bond lengths in those structures are 1.08 Å. The lengths of the C-C bonds at the edge and in the inner zone are 1.39 Å and 1.43 Å, respectively. Even though we increase the size, the bond angle between carbons remains constant at about 120°. Furthermore, as the size of functionalized CQDs increases, so does the planarity of the structure. The number of π-conjugated rings in all functionalized CQDs ranges from 4 to 9. The optimal carbon C-C bond length at the inner surface of COOH-functionalized CQDs stays constant at 1.42 Å, varies at the edges, and is consistent with previously reported findings [51].

The length of the optimized COOH-functionalized CQDs ranges from 7.36 to 12.26 Å. There are slight variations in the C-C-C bond angle in all CQDs functionalized with COOH groups. The C-O-H bond angle varies as the size of COOH-functionalized CQDs increases. The bond lengths between O-H, C-O, and CO atoms are optimized to be 0.96, 1.36, and 1.21 Å, respectively. These values are consistent with the bond lengths reported on COOH-functionalized graphene sheets [52]. The addition of COOH to CQDs causes a modification in their structure and this finding is consistent with the previous results on carboxylated graphene [53].

OH-functionalized CQDs have lengths in the range of 7.35–12.31 Å. The optimized structure of all OH-functionalized CQDs exhibits minimal alterations in the C-C-C and C-O-H bond angles as the size of the CQDs increases. The optimized bond lengths of 0.96 and 1.36 Å between the O-H and C-O atoms, respectively, are consistent with the bond lengths reported for the functionalized graphene [52,54]. In OH-functionalized CQDs, the bond length of C–C bonds varies at the edges but remains constant at the inner surface.

The NH2-functionalized CQDs exhibit a length ranging from 7.35 to 12.26 Å. The optimized structure of all NH2-functionalized CQDs exhibits minimal changes in the C-C-C bond angle. However, the C-N-H bond angle varies with the size of NH2-functionalized CQDs. The observed C-N-H bond angle (ranging from 110.39° to 121.15°) falls within the comparable range reported on 2-aminobenzimidazole [55]. The optimized bond length between the N-H and C-N atoms is 1.01 Å and 1.40 Å, respectively. These values are consistent with the previous reports on NH2-functionalized graphene-based nanostructures [56,57]. The C-C bond length at the inner surface of NH2-functionalized CQDs remains constant, while it varies at the edges.

SH-functionalized CQDs range in length from 7.35 to 12.33 Å. As the size of the SH-functionalized CQDs increases, the optimized structure of all of the CQDs demonstrates a variation in the C-C-C bond angle and the C-S-H bond angle. The C-S-H bond angle is consistent with the previous results, and the optimal bond length between the S-H bond is 1.34 Å [58]. The reported bond length for the C-S bond in cysteine and the optimized bond length of 1.78 Å for the C-S bond are in agreement [59].

The size of H2PO3-functionalized CQDs ranges from 7.34 to 12.25 Å. The optimized structure of all H2PO3-functionalized CQDs exhibits significant changes in the C-C-C bond angle, increasing by 1°. Additionally, the C-P-O bond angle increases to 105° and the P-O-H bond angle increases to 110° as the size of the H2PO3-functionalized CQDs increases. The P-O-H bond angle aligns with a previous study on 4-phenylpiperazine-1-ium dihydrogen phosphate [60]. The optimized bond lengths between the C-P, P-O, and P = O atoms are 1.81, 1.61, and 1.48 Å, respectively. These values are consistent with the bond lengths reported in recent studies on H2PO3-functionalized graphene and bisphosphonate compounds [61,62].

To summarise, a majority of functionalized CQDs exhibit a change in the planarity of their structure as they increase in size. However, the most significant change in planarity was found in the oxygen-containing functionalized CQDs, specifically those functionalized with OH, H2PO3, and COOH groups. The C23O13H13 molecule, which is an OH-functionalized CQD, exhibited the greatest increase in planarity with a value of 166.95°.

We observed that the formation energy of the modelled functionalized CQDs is negative and they are energetically stable. The details on the formation energy of all functionalized CQDs are provided in the SI (Table S2). The formation energy of COOH, OH, NH2, SH, and H2PO3 functionalized CQDs of various sizes is in the range of −6.02 to −5.29 eV, −5.23 to −4.53 eV, −6.00 to −5.23 eV, −5.67 to −0.43 eV, and −5.78 to −4.90 eV, respectively. The functionalized CQDs with the same number of atoms, such as the systems consisting of 31 (C18H11O2, C19H11S), 33 (C20H11O2, C17H12O3P), 39 (C24H13O2, C17O11H11, C25H13S), 41 (C26H13O2, C19O11H11, C23H14O3P), 47 (C30H15O2, C31H15S), 49 (C32H15O2, C23O13H13, C29H16O3P), 51(C25O13H13, C31H16O3P) atoms exhibited a significant disparity in their formation energy. Thus, the stability of the CQD is significantly influenced by its functional group. The COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) had the maximum formation energy of −6.02 eV, while the SH-functionalized CQD (C31H15S) had the lowest formation energy of −0.43 eV. Also, the lower formation energy suggests that the functionalization of CQDs with SH is energetically favorable. Experimentally, functionalized CQDs have been extensively studied and confirmed using atomic force microscopy and transmission electron microscopy [63–65].

The ESP plot is a crucial tool for identifying regions of a molecule that are deficient or excess in electrons. The alteration in the electron density observed in the ESP plots of all the functionalized CQDs can be attributed to the modification in the hybridization of carbon atoms, resulting in a change of the π-electron cloud in the carbon region [69]. The regions with high electron density are marked in red, whereas the areas with low electron density are in blue. The ESP plots of all functionalized CQDs are provided in the SI (Fig. S1).

Functionalizing with COOH results in a decrease in the electron density around the neighbouring carbon atoms. The CO bond in the COOH has a high electron density. As a result, this area is prone to electrophilic attack. The O atom, being very electronegative, creates a partially higher electron density around the C/H atom. This observation was also reported on functionalized carbonaceous material [70]. The H atom in the OH group attached to the carboxyl group is electron-deficient. This region exhibits active behavior and can be susceptible to nucleophilic attacks. Similar observations were reported on functionalized graphene oxide aerogel, and COOH-functionalized coronene [71,72]. The introduction of the OH functional group reduces the electron density in the surrounding carbon atoms. The OH group in the functionalized CQDs has an oxygen atom with a high electron density, while the H atom in the OH group is electron-deficient. This is similar to observations reported on graphene oxide [73]. Functionalizing CQDs with NH2 groups leads to a decrease in electron density in the carbon region, resulting in a non-uniform distribution of charge over the surface of the functionalized CQDs. The hydrogen atom of the NH2 group in the functionalized CQDs has an electron-deficient area, while the nitrogen atom of the NH2 group has a slightly higher electron density than hydrogen. A similar finding was reported on amine-functionalized graphene [74]. SH functionalization of CQDs decreases the electron density in the nearby carbon atoms and this agrees with the reports on SH-substituted oligo phenylene ethylene [75]. The functionalization of CQDs with the H2PO3 group leads to a reduction in the symmetric charge distribution in the carbon region. However, there is an asymmetry in the charge distribution on the surface of H2PO3 functionalized CQDs. The high electron density is concentrated on the oxygen atom that is doubly bonded to phosphorus in H2PO3. On the contrary, the H atom attached to the edges of functionalized CQDs has a lower electron density. A similar finding was reported on phosphate-functionalized carbon nanotubes [62].

Frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) such as HOMO, LUMO, and the energy gap between them play a crucial role in evaluating the chemical stability of a system. The system with the largest energy gap is more stable. Functionalized CQDs containing unpaired electrons result in a doublet state of spin. As a consequence, the FMOs split into alpha and beta molecular orbitals with distinct energy gaps [76,77]. Before the functionalization of CQDs, the energy gap of alpha and beta molecular orbitals lies in the range of 2.29–3.24 eV and 2.21–3.33 eV respectively which was reported in our earlier studies for rectangular CQDs [50]. Upon functionalization of CQDs, there is a shift in the energy levels of HOMO, and LUMO thus leading to a slight change in the energy gap. A maximum decrease in energy gap for alpha and beta molecular orbitals was observed for OH-functionalized CQDs C19O11H11 (0.56 eV) and C17O11H11 (0.37 eV) respectively. The lowest decrease in energy gap for alpha and beta molecular orbitals was obtained for SH-functionalized CQD C31H15S (0.003 eV) and OH-functionalized CQD C19O11H11 (0.004 eV). Invariable to the functional groups, as the size of the functionalized CQDs increased, the energy gap decreased due to the quantum confinement effect [74].

The incorporation of COOH groups into CQDs results in a decrease in the energy gap when compared to bare CQDs. The decrease in the energy gap may be attributed to the hybridization between the COOH functional group and CQD, which aligns with the previous findings of Feng et al., on COOH-functionalized graphene quantum dots [78]. The energy gap between alpha molecular orbitals ranges from 2.14 eV (C32H15O2) to 3.09 eV (C18H11O2). The stability of COOH-functionalized CQD for alpha molecular orbitals increases in the following sequence based on the energy gap:
The energy gap between the beta molecular orbitals ranges from 2.27 to 3.22 eV. A similar pattern was noted in the beta molecular orbitals of COOH functionalized CQD as well.

The introduction of OH groups onto CQDs results in a reduction in the energy gap. The decrease in the energy gap may be ascribed to the hybridization between the OH functional group and CQD, which is consistent with previous results on OH-functionalized graphene quantum dots [78]. The energy gap between the alpha molecular orbitals ranges from 1.86 eV (C31O15H15) to 2.69 eV (C17O11H11). Based on the energy gap, the OH-functionalized CQD for alpha molecular orbitals has the following ascending order of stability:

The energy gap of beta molecular orbitals in OH-functionalized CQDs varies as their size increases. The energy difference between the beta molecular orbitals ranges from 2.11 eV (C29O15H15) to 3.17 eV (C19O11H11). The order of stability for OH-functionalized CQD based on the energy gap of beta molecular orbitals is as follows:

The functionalization of CQDs with NH2 groups resulted in a decrease in the energy gap and an increase in the size of the functionalized CQDs. In NH2-functionalized CQDs, quantum confinement was found, and this observation was correlated with the functionalized CQDs' increasing size. The energy difference between the alpha molecular orbitals ranges from 2.30 (C31H16N) to 3.30 eV (C17H12N). The stability of NH2-functionalized CQDs for alpha molecular orbitals, based on the energy gap, follows the ascending order:
The energy gap of beta molecular orbitals ranges from 2.21 to 3.32 eV. A similar pattern was noted in NH2-functionalized CQDs for beta molecular orbitals as well.

The energy gap decreases as the size of SH-functionalized CQDs increases, particularly when the size of the dots increases as a result of quantum confinement. Alpha molecular orbitals have an energy gap ranging from 2.29 eV (C31H15S) to 3.16 eV (C17H11S). The stability of alpha molecular orbitals in SH-functionalized CQDs increases in the following order, based on the energy gap:
The energy gap for the beta molecular orbitals ranges from 2.19 to 3.30 eV. A similar pattern was found in SH-functionalized CQDs regarding the beta molecular orbitals as well.

The introduction of the H2PO3 group onto CQDs results in a reduction in the energy gap. The energy gap of H2PO3-functionalized CQDs exhibits change as its size increases. The energy gap of the alpha molecular orbitals ranges from 2.27 (C31H16O3P) to 3.22 eV (C17H12O3P). The stability of H2PO3-functionalized CQDs for alpha molecular orbitals increases in the following order, based on the energy gap:
C31H16O3P < C29H16O3P < C25H14O3P < C23H14O3P < C19H12O3P < C17H12O3P

The energy gap of the beta molecular orbitals ranges from 2.20 eV (C31H16O3P) to 3.19 eV (C19H12O3P). The stability of H2PO3-functionalized CQDs for beta molecular orbitals increases in the following order, based on the energy gap:
C31H16O3P < C29H16O3P < C25H14O3P < C23H14O3P < C17H12O3P < C19H12O3P

In conclusion, the energy gap decreases with increasing size for all functionalized CQDs. The energy gap ranges of the majority of functionalized CQDs are comparable, while OH-functionalized CQDs have the lowest energy gap range.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra suggest useful information for evaluating the absorbance of a molecule in a solution to obtain details on the transition of electrons between bonding and anti-bonding orbitals [79]. We obtained the UV-Vis absorption spectra of all modified CQDs in the water medium ( Fig. 2). Typically, as the size of functionalized CQDs increases, the energy gap decreases, resulting in a red shift in the characteristic peaks. This occurrence is a result of the quantum confinement effect.

Three distinct peaks are observed in the UV-Vis absorption spectra of carboxyl-functionalized CQDs. One of the peaks arises from the transition between the π to π* orbitals, while the other two peaks arise from the transition between the n to π* orbitals. The transition from π to π* occurs within the wavelength range of 245–318 nm, and is attributed to the existence of aromatic sp2 (CC) domains. The transition from the n to π* orbitals, occurring within the wavelength range of 375–600 nm, is attributed to the presence of the CO bond. A similar finding was reported on the passivated CQDs [80].

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of OH-functionalized CQDs exhibit two distinct peaks. The occurrence of these transitions is attributed to the π (bonding orbital) to π* (antibonding orbital) and n (non-bonding orbital) to π* transitions. Zhang et al., observed that in the case of graphene quantum dots (QDs), the π to π* transition takes place within the wavelength range of 285–349 nm for OH-functionalized CQDs [81]. The reason for this change is the existence of aromatic sp2 (CC) domains. The transition from the n to π* state appears within the 348–530 nm wavelength range in OH-functionalized CQDs. This occurrence is attributed to the existence of the C-O group. Similar findings were reported on CQDs with oxygen functional groups and OH-functionalized graphene QD, respectively [82,83].

The introduction of NH2 groups to CQDs results in two distinct peaks. The transitions responsible for this occurrence are π to π* and n to π* transitions. Similar observations on functionalized carbon dots have been reported [84]. The transition from π to π* takes place within the wavelength range of 235–322 nm due to the existence of aromatic sp2 (CC) domains. The n to π* transition occurs at wavelengths below 378–650 nm, and could be as a result of the C-N bond. This observation aligns with previous findings on functionalized nitrogen-doped CQDs [82].

The absorption spectrum of CQDs functionalized with the SH group exhibits distinct peaks that can be attributed to π to π* and n to π* transitions in a water medium. The presence of aromatic sp2 (CC) domains resulted in the observation of π to π* transition within the wavelength range of 242–327 nm. This phenomenon was reported on nitrogen-doped SH-functionalized carbon dots [85]. The occurrence of n to π* transition was identified within the wavelength range of 378–651 nm.

The H2PO3-functionalized CQDs in water exhibit distinct peaks in their UV-Vis absorption spectrum, which can be attributed to π to π* and n to π* transitions. The π to π* transition was observed in the wavelength range of 239–320 nm, which is attributed to the presence of aromatic sp2 (CC) domains. A similar observation was reported on phosphate-functionalized carbon nanodots [86]. The occurrence of n to π* transition was found throughout the wavelength range of 371–662 nm. This transition is attributed to the C-P bond, which is consistent with previous findings on CQDs co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorous [87].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction studies of toxic metals with functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For studying the interaction of toxic metals, larger-sized functionalized CQDs (C32H15O2, C31H15O15, C31H16N, C19H11S, and C31H16O3P) are chosen since they possess greater structural stability based on formation energy. Furthermore, the investigations are performed in both gaseous and aqueous phases. Following the adsorption of metal, noticeable changes in the lengths of chemical bonds, angles between bonds, and the flatness of some functionalized CQDs were observed. However, there are only minimal changes observed in the lengths of C-H, C-C, C-P, P-O, P = O, N-H, C-O, CO, C-N, O-H, C-S, and S-H bonds. On the other hand, there are noticeable changes in the bond angles of C-O-H (0.06° to 1.59°), C-N-H (6.77° to 11.57°), C-P-O (0.61° to 6.17°), P-O-H (0.14° to 5.28°), and C-S-H (0.01° to 1.42°) after the formation of a metal complex in functionalized CQDs. The C-C-C bond angle undergoes minimal alteration, hence maintaining its planarity following the adsorption of the metal. Fig. 3 shows the functionalized CQDs+metal complexes having high adsorption energies. The rest of the optimized structures of functionalized CQDs+metal complexes has been provided in Figure S2. The adsorption energies are reported in Table S3.

The Fe adsorption energies in functionalized CQDs range from −2.94 to −3.73 eV, indicating that Fe chemisorbs onto these structures. The presence of Fe in the NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) has resulted in a significant alteration of the C-N-H bond angle, increasing it by approximately 11.57°. The distance of interaction between Fe and O, which is 2.06 Å, is in close agreement with the bond length of the Fe-O bond in the Fe-phosphate complex system [88]. A significant amount of charge transfer is taking place in the SH-functionalized CQD with Fe, specifically in the compound C19H11S+Fe (0.21 e). The H2PO3-functionalized CQD containing Fe (C31H16O3P +Fe) shows the highest adsorption energy (-3.73 eV) compared to other Fe-adsorbed functionalized CQDs. This suggests that Fe chemically bonds to the substrate by chemisorption. Here, the overlap of valence orbitals occurs between the lone pairs (2p) of oxygen and the 3d/4 s orbitals of iron. However, the primary contribution comes from the 3d orbital of iron, which is the cause of the interaction between the Fe and the H2PO3-functionalized CQD [88]. The OH-functionalized CQD with Fe (C31O15H15+Fe) has the lowest adsorption energy of −2.94 eV among the Fe-adsorbed functionalized CQDs.

The adsorption energy of Cr on functionalized CQDs ranges from −2.64 to −4.02 eV, while the interaction distance ranges from 2.00 to 2.55 Å. These values suggest that Cr is chemisorbed onto the surface of the CQDs. The adsorption energy of the system including NH2-functionalized CQD with Cr (C31H16N + Cr) is the highest among all functionalized CQDs, measuring −4.02 eV. Lin et al., have obtained a similar observation using Cr-embedded nitrogen-doped graphene. This is due to the stronger interactions between Cr and N, resulting from the orbital interaction between Cr's 4 s and N's 2p orbitals [89]. Furthermore, the difference in electronegativity between Cr and N is a contributing factor [90]. The OH-functionalized CQD with Cr (C31O15H15+Cr) has the lowest adsorption energy of −2.64 eV among the Cr-adsorbed functionalized CQDs. The C-N-H bond angle has been altered by approximately 10.77° as a result of the strong bonding between Cr and NH2-functionalized CQD C31H16N. The distance of interaction between Cr and N, which is 2.17 Å, corresponds to the bond length of the Cr-N bond seen in dichromium compounds. This similarity was observed during the studies on the interaction of vanillin with Cr [90,91].

The chemisorption of Se in functionalized CQDs is demonstrated by its adsorption energy range of −1.82 to −2.46 eV and interaction distance range of 2.00–3.64 Å. The CQD system, which has been NH2-functionalized with Se (C31H16N + Se), has the most significant adsorption energy of −2.46 eV. The interaction between Selenium (Se) and the NH2-functionalized CQD is mainly caused by the orbital interaction between the lone pair of nitrogen (N) and the antibonding orbital of Se's 4p subshell. An equivalent result was reported on organoselenium compounds [92]. The metal forms a strong bond with the nitrogen atom of C31H16N, with an interaction distance of 2.06 Å, which matches the bond length observed on tetraatomic Se-N complexes [93]. The NH2-functionalized CQD and Se (C31H16N + Se) exhibit a strong interaction, resulting in a notable alteration of the C-N-H bond angle (11.44°). A modest amount of charge transfer occurs in H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Se, C31H16O3P + Se (0.28 e). Se exhibits the lowest adsorption energy (-1.82 eV) on OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15+Se).

The obtained adsorption energies (‐0.01 to 0.01 eV) and interaction distances (3.34–4.92 Å) suggest that Hg is physisorbed onto functionalized CQDs. The NH2-functionalized CQD with mercury (C31H16N + Hg) exhibits the highest adsorption energy (-0.37 eV). The OH-functionalized CQD with Hg (C31O15H15+Hg) has the lowest adsorption energy (-0.01 eV). The interaction between Hg and functionalized CQDs is weaker because of the larger bond lengths. The SH-functionalized CQD with Hg (C19H11S+Hg) has a positive adsorption energy value, suggesting that Hg does not undergo spontaneous adsorption. Earlier findings identified that the vacant molecular orbitals of NH2-functionalized CQD interact with the outermost valence electrons of Hg (mercury), which accounts for the higher interaction between Hg and the nitrogen atom of NH2-functionalized CQD [74]. An observable variation in the C-N-H bond angle (6.77°) is seen in Hg when interacting with NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N + Hg). A small amount of charge transfer takes place in H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Hg, namely C31H16O3P + Hg (0.18 e).

The range of adsorption energy (‐0.25 to 0.01 eV) and interaction distance (2.17–2.52 Å) of Sb on functionalized CQDs indicates that Sb is physisorbed onto the surface. The NH2-functionalized CQD with Sb (C31H16N + Sb) showed the highest adsorption energy (-0.53 eV). The SH-functionalized CQD with Sb (C19H11S+Sb) has the lowest adsorption energy (-0.25 eV) in the system. Sb has a stronger interaction with the nitrogen (N) atom of NH2-functionalized CQD because of the formation of a co-ordinate bond between the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom of the NH2 group and the antimony (Sb) atom. A similar observation was reported on sulfamethazine regarding the adsorption of antimony [89]. The distance of interaction between Sb and N, which is 2.35 Å, is similar to the previously reported bond length of Sb-N, which was 2.21 Å [94]. The OH-functionalized CQD with Sb (C31O15H15+Sb) has a positive adsorption energy value of 0.01 eV, suggesting that spontaneous adsorption of Sb does not occur in the gas phase. The presence of NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N + Sb) causes a notable alteration in the C-N-H bond angle (10.82°) in Sb due to their interaction. The charge transfer between functionalized CQDs and Sb is minimal, ranging from 0.01 to 0.14 e.

The adsorption energy (-0.005 to −0.38 eV) and interaction distance (3.15–4.64 Å) of Cd on functionalized CQDs indicate that Cd is physically adsorbed onto the surface of the CQDs. The NH2-functionalized CQD with Cd (C31H16N + Cd) exhibits the highest adsorption energy of −0.38 eV. The higher adsorption energy can be attributed to the interaction between the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom of the NH2 group and cadmium. A similar observation was made on amidoxime-chelating cellulose during the adsorption of cadmium [95]. The system SH-functionalized CQD with Cd (C19H11S+Cd) has the lowest adsorption energy (-0.005 eV). The interaction between NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) with Cd (C31H16N + Cd) causes a notable alteration in the C-N-H bond angle (6.92°) in Cd when compared with NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N). The charge transfer is minimal when functionalized CQDs interact with Cd, ranging from 0.04 to 0.21 e.

In conclusion, the majority of functionalized CQDs exhibit the strongest adsorption towards Cr followed by Fe and Se. Thus, this kind of chemisorption of metals on functionalized CQDs ensures them to be employed as filters or absorbents [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption studies of toxic metals with functionalized CQDs in the water phase
ce:section.ce:para: This section reports the variations in the formation energy (Table S2), adsorption energy (Table S3), and dipole moment when functionalized CQDs interact with toxic metals in water. Tables S2 and S3 have been provided in the SI. There is a significant alteration in the dipole moments of all functionalized CQDs following the adsorption of toxic metals. The complex exhibited an increase in its dipole moment as a result of alterations in the charge distribution caused by the influence of water [96]. Furthermore, the computed formation energies of functionalized CQD+metal complexes have negative values, indicating the stability of their structures in the aqueous phase.

The COOH-functionalized CQD with Fe (C32H15O2+Fe) exhibits a significant 37.84 Debye increase in dipole moment. The OH-functionalized CQD with Fe (C31O15H15+Fe) exhibits the smallest change in dipole moment (21.92 Debye). The H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Fe (C31H16O3P + Fe) exhibited the highest adsorption energy (-4.16 eV) in the aqueous phase. A similar pattern was seen for the adsorption energy of this system in the gas phase as well. The lowest adsorption energy (-2.92 eV) was observed for OH-functionalized CQD with Fe (C31O15H15+Fe).

A notable change in dipole moment is found in NH2-functionalized CQD with Cr (C31H16N + Cr), equivalent to approximately 32.84 Debye. Conversely, the dipole moment of OH-functionalized CQD with Cr (C31O15H15+Cr) in the aqueous phase exhibits the smallest change by about 20.53 Debye. The COOH-functionalized CQD with Cr (C32H15O2+Cr) has the highest adsorption energy in the water phase (-4.33 eV). The system OH-functionalized CQD with Cr, C31O15H15+Cr has the lowest adsorption energy (-3.73 eV) in the water phase.

An appreciable variation in dipole moment is evident in OH-functionalized CQD with Se (C31O15H15+Se), measuring around 28.05 Debye, while the dipole moment change is minimal in NH2-functionalized CQD with Se (C31H16N + Se) measuring around 1.59 Debye. In the water phase, the system OH-functionalized CQD with Se (C31O15H15+Se) showed the maximum adsorption energy of −2.44 eV. The lowest adsorption energy (-1.71 eV) was observed for COOH-functionalized CQD with Se (C32H15O2+Se).

The H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Hg (C31H16O3P + Hg) shows a noticeable decrease in the dipole moment of 2.16 Debye, but it is slightly lower than in the gas phase. The COOH-functionalized CQD with Hg (C32H15O2+Hg) compound shows the least significant alteration in dipole moment caused by water (0.20 Debye). In the water phase, the system H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Hg (C31H16O3P + Hg) has the highest adsorption energy of −0.38 eV. However, in the gas phase, the system NH2-functionalized with Hg (C31H16N + Hg), which is NH2-functionalized, has the highest adsorption energy of −0.37 eV. Remarkably, the interaction between Hg and SH-functionalized CQD (C19H11S+Hg) results in negative adsorption energy when in the water phase. The system SH-functionalized CQD with Hg, C19H11S+Hg has the lowest adsorption energy (-0.23 eV) in the water phase.

The OH-functionalized CQD with Sb (C31O15H15+Sb) exhibits the highest change in dipole moment, measuring approximately 30.73 Debye. Conversely, the NH2-functionalized CQD with Sb (C31H16N + Sb), shows the lowest change in dipole moment due to the influence of water (5.02 Debye). The OH-functionalized CQD with Sb (C31O15H15+Sb) compound shows the highest adsorption energy (-0.24 eV) in the water phase. The lowest adsorption energy (-0.06 eV) was observed for NH2-functionalized CQD with Sb, C31H16N + Sb in the water phase.

The presence of water causes a notable alteration in the dipole moment of H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Cd (C31H16O3P + Cd), resulting in a change of approximately 30.33 Debye. However, the dipole moment of SH-functionalized CQD with Cd (C19H11S+Cd) exhibits the least change (0.47 Debye). In the aqueous phase, the H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Cd (C31H16O3P + Cd) exhibited the highest adsorption energy of −0.38 eV. The system, COOH-functionalized CQD with Cd (C32H15O2+Cd) shows the lowest adsorption energy of −0.14 eV.

In summary, we can observe that the magnitude of adsorption of the toxic metals ranges from physisorption to strong chemisorption towards the functionalized CQDs. The difference in adsorption energies is due to the following reasons:

The functional groups possess different chemical properties. The OH group present in the hydroxyl-functionalized and carboxyl-functionalized CQD is highly electronegative compared to amino-functionalized CQD [36]. The thiol group has a strong nucleophilic nature which can donate electrons during interaction [97]. The amino group has an electron-donating nature with nitrogen possessing a lone pair of electrons [98]. The phosphono group is electron-donating and it is highly ionic and low in electronegativity [99].

Moreover, the metals have different electronic configurations which consists of half-filled and completely filled atomic orbitals. Accordingly, their magnitude of interaction will vary too. For instance, the presence of vacant 3d orbitals in Fe and Cr is the reason for their higher adsorption with the electron donating functional groups present in the CQDs. However, in the case of metals such as Hg (4 f145d104 s2) and Cd (4d105 s2) the outermost orbitals are filled hence adsorption energy with the functionalized CQDs is smaller. This is in line with earlier report on graphene oxide [39]. The isosurfaces of the HOMOs and LUMOs orbitals can be used to interpret the orbital interactions between the functionalized CQDs and metals. It was observed that the HOMOs are mainly concentrated on the plane of the functionalized CQDs while LUMOs are largely present on the metals, this indicates the hybridization between the functionalized CQDs and metals [100]. In addition, lesser energy gap between the molecular orbitals of metals (Fe, Cr) and the functionalized CQDs leads to a larger degree of hybridization than other metals [100]. The orbital interaction occurs between 3d orbitals of metals (Fe and Cr) and 2p orbitals of oxygen/nitrogen or 3p orbitals of sulphur in the functionalized CQDs [98,101–103]. For metals such as Hg, Cd, Se, Sb, the orbital interaction could occur between their s and d orbitals, and 2p orbital of oxygen/nitrogen and the 3p orbital of sulphur in the functionalized CQDs [103]. The magnitude of adsorption energy has a dependence on the interaction distance, the smaller the distance, the higher the adsorption energy. This is in agreement with earlier findings reported for graphene and graphene oxide towards the detection of Cr and Cd, Hg, respectively [39,42,100,104].

Further, we have made compared our results on functionalized CQDs with other materials investigated for toxic metals detection using DFT calculations ( Table 1). The adsorption energy range of Cr towards the functionalized CQDs is in the range of −3.73 to −4.33 eV. It is very high and remarkable compared to the earlier findings on the detection of Cr using carboxymethyl cellulose metal-organic framework (MOF) and graphene, respectively [41,42]. The adsorption energy of Se on these functionalized CQDs ranges from −1.71 to −2.19 eV, demonstrating superior adsorption performance compared to the findings reported by Wei et al. for UiO-66 MOF and amino-functionalized UiO-66 MOF [43]. Cadmium exhibits a higher adsorption energy of −0.38 eV with phosphono-functionalized CQD (C31H16O3P), which is marginally greater than the adsorption energy reported on graphene during the interaction studies with Se [42]. The adsorption performance of the phosphono-functionalized CQD (C31H16O3P) for Hg is superior to the adsorption energy value reported for graphene during interaction studies with Hg [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of toxic metals using UV-Vis absorption spectra in water phase
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis absorption spectra of functionalized CQDs with metals (chromium, iron, selenium, cadmium, antimony, and mercury) in the presence of water have been obtained ( Fig. 4). When metals are adsorbed onto functionalized CQDs, the absorbance peaks in the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the functionalized CQDs can either decrease or increase. The percentage of change is determined by quantifying the variation in peak intensity before and after metal complexation and the data is provided in Table S5.

A decrease in the intensity of the absorption peak is observed when COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) interacts with metals such as Cr, Fe, Se, Cd, Sb, and Hg. This occurrence arises from the transfer of charge between functionalized CQDs and metals, as well as the strong binding between the surface groups (COOH) of COOH-functionalized CQDs and metals [36]. Similarly, fluorescence quenching has been reported when Cr (VI), Fe3+, and Hg2+ ions were adsorbed onto COOH-functionalized CQDs [29–31]. The absorbance of metals decreases by a percentage ranging from 8.42 % to 69.91 % when exposed to COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2). The largest decrease in absorbance was obtained for iron (Fe) when interacting with C32H15O2, with a percentage decrease of 69.91 %. The observed decrease in absorbance is consistent with the greater adsorption energy of COOH-functionalized CQD with Fe and Cr. Furthermore, the interaction between metals and COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) leads to a shift of the peak. There has been a red shift in all metals, causing the highest absorption peak to shift from 322 to 508 nm. A similar observation has been reported on salicylaldehyde derivatives towards the detection of Cu2+ ions [105]. The redshift may result from the reduction in the energy gap of COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) upon interaction with metals.

A reduction in the intensity of the absorption peak is observed when OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15) is combined with metals such as chromium (Cr), iron (Fe), and selenium (Se). It arises as a result of charge transfer and adsorption between functionalized CQDs and metals [36]. A similar observation about fluorescence quenching in OH-functionalized CQDs due to the adsorption of Fe3+ and Cr6+ ions has been reported. This was observed in the fluorescence emission spectra [28,30]. We have observed that the absorption of metals decreases by a percentage ranging from 9.11 % to 44.56 % in OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15). The system C31O15H15+Se showed the highest decrease in intensity, with a percentage of 44.56 %. Moreover, for metals Hg, Sb and Cd upon interaction with C31O15H15, there is an increase in the peak intensity of about 3.31 %,28.79 %, and 8.83 % in the UV-Vis absorption spectra. Furthermore, the interaction between C31O15H15 and metals primarily leads to a displacement of the peak. There has been a red shift in the absorption peak of Cr, Fe, Se, Cd, and Hg, causing it to shift from 368 to 520 nm. A similar result was reported on salicylaldehyde derivatives for the detection of Cu2+ ions [105]. Remarkably, the OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15) exhibits a blue shift when interacting with Sb (C31O15H15+Sb). The redshift may result from the reduction in the energy gap of C31O15H15 following its adsorption with metals.

The percentage of absorption reduction for metals that have been functionalized using NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) is within the range of 89.64–98.42 %. The Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) exhibited the highest percentage reduction in absorbance, reaching 98.42 %. The adsorption energy of NH2-functionalized CQD with Fe and Cr is greater, indicating a significant reduction. This occurrence arises as a result of the strong chemical interaction between NH2-functionalized CQD and metals [36]. The results of our study, which involved measuring the decrease in absorbance to detect metals, align with the previously reported findings. In their research, they also used NH2-functionalized CQD to detect Cr6+ ions, from fluorescence emission spectra [28,32]. When metals interact with C31H16N, it causes a shift in the peak. There has been a red shift in all metals, as seen by the shift of the highest absorption peak from 317 to 460 nm in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum. A similar finding was reported for the detection of Cu2+ ions using salicylaldehyde derivatives [105]. The redshift may be attributed to the reduction in the energy gap of C31H16N following its adsorption with metals.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of metals (Cr, Fe, Se, Sb, and Hg) show a drop in absorption percentage when interacting with SH-functionalized CQD (C19H11S), ranging from 25.03 % to 39.16 %. The greatest decrease in intensity was obtained for SH-functionalized CQD with Cr (C19H11S+Cr) of 39.16 %. The interaction of Cd with C19H11S has resulted in a peak intensity increment of about 50.24 %. A significant reduction in absorbance in the UV-Vis spectrum is in line with the observed trend in adsorption energy exhibited by SH-functionalized CQD with Fe and Cr. This reduction occurs due to charge transfer between metal and SH-functionalized CQD C19H11S and also due to the complex formation between the SH-functionalized CQD and metals [36]. The results of our study on the detection of Fe and Hg using SH-functionalized CQD are consistent with the findings reported on L-cysteine functionalized carbon dot towards the detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ using fluorescence spectra [34]. The interaction of metals with SH-functionalized CQD causes a redshift from 260 to 424 nm in the UV-Vis absorption spectrum. A similar finding was reported on salicylaldehyde derivatives to identify Cu2+ ions [105]. The redshift may result from the reduction in the energy gap of (C19H11S) after interaction with metals.

The percentage decrease in intensity for metals treated with H2PO3-functionalized CQD (C31H16O3P) varies from 15.49 % to 71.48 %. The intensity change reduction percentage for Fe with C31H16O3P is 71.48 %. The adsorption energy trend exhibited by H2PO3-functionalized CQD with Fe and Cr correlates with the significant reduction in absorbance observed in the spectrum. The decrease in intensity can be attributed to the development of a complex between the probe (H2PO3-functionalized CQD) and the quencher (metal) [36]. The results of our study, which involved the use of H2PO3-functionalized CQD (C31H16O3P), are consistent with the prior findings reported on phosphate-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride QDs towards the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions using the photoluminescence spectra [35]. Additionally, the interaction between C31H16O3P and metals leads to a displacement of the peak. There has been a red shift in all metals, as indicated by the shift of the maximum absorbance peak from 320 to 473 nm. A similar finding was reported on salicylaldehyde derivatives during the detection of Cu2+ ions using the UV-Vis absorption spectrum [105]. The redshift may be attributed to the reduction in the energy gap of C31H16O3P following its interaction with metals.

Experimentally, the detection of hazardous metal ions in aqueous environments using functionalized CQDs have been reported using fluorescence quenching technique [28,29,32]. As shown in Table 2, there has been not much investigation on the use of UV–visible absorption spectroscopy for metal ion detection using functionalized CQDs, spanning both experimental and DFT investigations [106]. However, our work has demonstrated metals detection utilizing UV-Vis absorption spectra through DFT calculations very extensively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of toxic metals using Raman spectra of functionalized CQD with metals
Raman Spectra Analysis of functionalized CQDs with metals
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral range of 0–3500 cm−1 is taken into account while analyzing the Raman spectra of functionalized CQD with and without metals ( Fig. 5). The bands within the spectral range of 1300–1700 cm−1 exhibit high intensity in all functionalized CQDs with metals. Two significant bands, D and G, can be observed. The D band is attributed to defects/disorder, whereas the G band is associated with sp2 vibrations between carbon atoms. The 2 G band in the Raman spectrum is a second-order characteristic that arises from a combination of modes or overtones [108]. The presence of 2 G mode was detected in all functionalized CQDs beyond 3100 cm−1 both before and after adsorption with metals. This mode is attributed to the vibrations of C-H bonds in the functionalized CQD. A similar result was reported on graphene QD [108]. During the process of adsorbing functionalized CQD with metals, new peaks appear, and the intensity of the bands’ changes. These new peaks serve as indicators of the presence of metals. Shtepliuk et al., showed observations on the identification of metals by the emergence of new peaks in Raman spectra, and these observations were made during the adsorption studies of heavy metals (Cd and Hg) with graphene QD [109].

The Raman spectra of C32H15O2 exhibit two distinct bands, namely D and G, which emerge at wavelengths of 1365 and 1596 cm−1, respectively. These findings align with previous findings reported on COOH-functionalized graphene QD [110]. The Raman spectra of C32H15O2 with metals exhibit two distinct bands, namely D and G, which are observed within the wavelength range of 1344–1365 cm−1 and 1575–1638 cm−1, respectively. Despite the adsorption of C32H15O2 with metals, there is no shift observed in the 2 G band. New peaks were identified in the Raman spectra for C32H15O2+Cr and C32H15O2+Fe in the wavelength ranges of 2856–2996 cm−1 and 3409–3493 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibrations for the Fe-O bond were observed at 609 and 637 cm−1, they agree with the previous reports on Fe-based complexes [111]. Stretching frequencies corresponding to the Cr-O bond were observed between 749 and 1200 cm−1, they are in line with previously reported observations on chromium oxide [112]. For the Se-O bond, the stretching vibrations were obtained at 840 and 930 cm−1, this matches well with earlier findings on selenate [113]. At 467 and 497 cm−1, Hg-O vibrations were observed, this is in line with the earlier reports on mercury complex [114]. For the Sb-O bond, the stretching vibrations were observed in the region between 400 and 750 cm−1, this matches with observations on klebelsbergite [115]. The stretching vibrations corresponding to the Cd-O bond were observed at 287 and 386 cm−1, this is in agreement with previously reported observations on cysteinate glycinate cadmium(II) complex [116]. Distinctive peaks for C32H15O2 in the presence of antimony (Sb) were seen at wavelengths ranging from 2800 to 2996 cm−1 and from 3409 to 3416 cm−1, respectively. Peaks in the spectral range of 1827–1925 cm−1 were found to be absent in C32H15O2+Fe, C32H15O2+Cr, C32H15O2+Se, C32H15O2+Sb, and C32H15O2+Cd, when compared to the Raman spectrum of C32H15O2.

The Raman spectra of C31H15O15 include two clearly distinguishable bands, known as D and G, which are detected at wavelengths of 1288 and 1589 cm−1, respectively. These findings align with previously reported observations [83,117]. The Raman spectra of C31H15O15 with metals exhibit the D and G bands within the wavelength range of 1288–1295 cm−1, and 1589 cm−1, respectively. New peaks were identified in the Raman spectra at a wavelength of 3318 cm−1 for C31H15O15+Cr and C31H15O15+Fe. New peaks were found in the wavelength range of 2513–2828 cm−1 and 3276–3318 cm−1 for the complex C31H15O15+Se. Additional peaks were seen within the wavelength range of 1946–2261 cm−1 and 3276–3318 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum of C31H15O15+Sb. The stretching vibrations of the Fe-O bond were recorded at 588, and 658 cm−1, consistent with prior findings on Fe-based complexes [111]. Stretching frequencies associated with the Cr-O bond were detected between 770 and 1200 cm−1, corroborating earlier reports on chromium oxide [112]. The vibrations of the Se-O bond were recorded at 801, 848, 884, and 979 cm−1, consistent with previous reports on selenate [113]. Hg-O stretching vibrations were seen at 416,451,479 and 501 cm−1, corroborating previously reported observations on mercury complex [114]. The stretching vibrations of the Sb-O bond were recorded at 418,453,480 and 697 cm−1, consistent with the findings on klebelsbergite [115].The stretching vibrations associated with the Cd-O bond were identified between 270 and 360 cm−1, corroborating previous findings on cysteinate glycinate cadmium(II) complex [116]. The Raman spectra of C31H15O15+Cd and C31H15O15+Hg do not exhibit the appearance of new peaks, but there is a noticeable alteration in the intensity of distinctive bands.

The Raman spectra of C31H16N exhibit two distinct bands, namely D and G, which are observed at wavelengths of 1365 and 1596 cm−1, respectively. These findings align with previous reports on NH2-functionalized graphene QD [118]. The Raman spectra of C31H16N with metals exhibit the D and G bands within the wavelength range of 1337–1372 cm−1 and 1589–1603 cm−1, respectively. There is a change in the frequency of the 2 G band after the C31H16N interacts with metals within the wavelength range of 3178–3192 cm−1. Additional peaks were detected in the Raman spectra of C31H16N + Se and C31H16N + Hg within the wavelength range of 1827–1834 cm−1 and 1820–1827 cm−1, respectively. New peaks were detected for C31H16N + Cr and C31H16N + Fe within the frequency range of 3360–3458 cm−1. New peaks for C31H16N + Cd were detected in the Raman spectra throughout the wavelength range of 1820–1827 cm−1, 3402–3458 cm−1, and 2989 cm−1. For the Fe-N bond, the stretching vibrations were observed between 200 and 300 cm−1, a similar finding was reported on the Fe-based polymer complex [119]. The stretching vibrations corresponding to the Cr-N bond are observed at 301,364 and 1015 cm−1, this is in line with earlier findings on Cr–ZSM-5 catalysts [120]. The band at 1015 cm−1 for the complex of C31H16N + Cr is more intense compared to the Raman spectra of C31H16N before interaction with metals. For the Se-N bond, the stretching vibrations were obtained at 396,457,473 cm−1, this matches well with the earlier observations on selenium-based ionic compounds [121]. The vibrations corresponding to the Cd-N bond were obtained at 396,416,478 cm−1, this agrees with earlier reported observations on cadmium(II)–cysteinate glycinate complex [116].

The Raman spectra of C19H11S display two separate bands, known as D and G, with wavelengths of 1344 and 1582 cm−1, respectively. The Raman spectra of C19H11S with metals exhibit the D and G bands within the wavelength range of 1330–1351 cm−1 and 1568–1617 cm−1, respectively. The interaction between metals and C19H11S leads to a shift of the 2 G band within the wavelength range of 3178–3185 cm−1. The Raman spectra for C19H11S +Cr and C19H11S+Fe revealed additional peaks at wavelengths of 3353 cm−1 and 2422–2520 cm−1, respectively. Additional spectral peaks for the compound C19H11S+Se were detected throughout the wavelength ranges of 1792–1813 cm−1, 2933–2996 cm−1, and 2520 cm−1. Additional peaks were seen in the Raman spectra of C19H11S+Sb within the wavelength intervals of 1792–1799 cm−1, 2422–2520 cm−1, and at 2996 cm−1. Similarly, the compound C19H11S+Hg exhibited additional peaks throughout the wavelength range of 1792–1827 cm−1. Upon comparing the Raman spectrum of C19H11S+Sb to that of C19H11S, it was observed that the peaks in the spectral range of 2744–2835 cm−1 vanished. For the Fe-S bond, the stretching vibrations were obtained at 217, 294,453 cm−1, and a similar observation was reported on Fe–S proteins [122].In the frequency range between 500 and 1000 cm−1, the bands are intense for the complex of C19H11S+Fe compared to the Raman spectrum of C19H11S. The stretching vibration corresponding to the Cr-S bond was obtained at 455 cm−1, this agrees with the earlier findings on a complex of potassium dichromate with tripeptide glutathione [123]. At frequencies 196.18, 205.53,293.77, and 329 cm−1 the stretching vibrations corresponding to the Se-S bond were obtained, this is in line with previous observations reported on ternary As–S–Se glass [124]. The stretching vibrations corresponding to the Hg-S bond were obtained at 296,325,457,471,527 cm−1, it agrees with earlier findings on cinnabar [125]. At 304 and 336 cm−1, the stretching vibrations of the Sb-S bond were obtained, it matches well with previous observations reported on SbS3 compounds [126]. For the Cd-S bond at 294 and 322 cm−1 the stretching vibrations were obtained, this observation is comparable to earlier findings on cadmium(II)–cysteinate glycinate complex [116].

The Raman spectra of C31H16O3P exhibit two unique bands, namely D and G, which have been observed at wavelengths of 1365 and 1596 cm−1, respectively. Due to adsorption between H2PO3-functionalized CQD and metals, new peaks arise and the intensity of the bands also varies, thereby revealing the existence of metals. The Raman spectra of C31H16O3P with metals exhibit the D and G bands, which are observed at wavelengths ranging from 1323 to 1365 cm−1 and 1585–1596 cm−1, respectively. When C31H16O3P interacts with metals, there is a shift in the 2 G band within the wavelength range of 3178–3192 cm−1. The Raman spectra revealed the appearance of additional peaks for C31H16O3P + Fe, C31H16O3P + Se, and C31H16O3P + Cd within specific wavelength ranges. These ranges were 3353–3493 cm−1, 3395–2493 cm−1, and 3353–3374 cm−1, respectively. New peaks were detected at wavelengths ranging from 2975 to 2996 cm−1, 3353–3367 cm−1, and 1813 cm−1 for the compound C31H16O3P + Sb. Additionally, peaks were observed at 1813 cm−1 and 2996 cm−1 for the compound C31H16O3P + Cr. The stretching vibrations for the Fe-O bond were observed at 483,567,616,623 and 637 cm−1, they are in line with the previous reports on Fe-based complexes [111]. Stretching frequencies corresponding to the Cr-O bond were observed between 800 and 1200 cm−1, they agree with previously reported observations on chromium oxide [112]. For the Se-O bond, the stretching vibrations were obtained at 643,709,817 and 854 cm−1, this is in line with earlier findings on selenate [113]. In the frequency region 413–469 cm−1 and at 383–388 cm−1, Hg-O vibrations were obtained, this matches well with the earlier reports on mercury complex [114]. For the Sb-O bond, the stretching vibrations were obtained in the region between 400 and 710 cm−1, this is in agreement with the previously reported observations on klebelsbergite [115]. The stretching vibrations corresponding to the Cd-O bond were found at 294 and 329 cm−1, this agrees well with previously reported observations on cysteinate glycinate cadmium(II) complex [116].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic moment of functionalized CQD with metals in the gas phase
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials with higher magnetic moment can enhance sensing ability and improve sensitivity towards analytes, making them suitable for magnetic bio-sensing applications [127]. The adsorption investigations have demonstrated a significant enhancement in the magnetic moment's strength, indicating a strong binding between functionalized CQDs and metals [128]. The calculated magnetic moment of functionalized CQDs ranges from 4.35 to 5.26 µB. The OH-functionalized CQD exhibited the highest magnetic moment of 5.26 µB, whereas the NH2-functionalized CQD had the lowest magnetic moment of 4.35 µB. The magnetic moment order is consistent with previous reported findings on graphene oxide and NH2-functionalized graphene QDs [129,130]. The calculated magnetic moment of functionalized CQD with metal complexes has been provided in Table 3.

The COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) has a magnetic moment ranging from 4.55 to 4.78 µB after metal adsorption. When C32H15O2 is adsorbed with metals, there is an observed increase in the magnetic moment. The largest change in the magnetic moment was recorded for the compound C32H15O2+Fe, with a magnitude of around 0.23 µB. The order of magnetic moment of the complex is consistent with the previous reports on graphene oxide with Fe [131]. The smallest variation in magnetic moment is observed in C32H15O2+Sb, with a value of 0.009 µB.

The magnetic moment of OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15) with metals varies between 5.25 and 5.43 µB. These findings suggest that the interaction between C31O15H15 and metals leads to an augmentation in the magnetic moment, except for C31O15H15+Sb, where the magnetic moment of the complex is insignificantly decreased. The C31O15H15+Fe complex displayed the highest increase in magnetic moment, by about 0.17 µB. The magnetic moment of the complex is consistent with the results reported on graphene oxide with Fe [131]. The change in the magnetic moment is minimal for C31O15H15+Se (0.01 µB).

The magnetic moment of the NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) with metals ranges from 4.36 to 4.55 µB. This indicates that the magnetic moment increases due to the adsorption between C31H16N and metals. The C31H16N + Fe complex exhibited the most significant increase in magnetic moment, measuring at 0.24 µB. The system, C31H16N + Sb demonstrates the most minimal change in magnetic moment, with a shift of about 0.03 µB.

The CQD (C19H11S) containing SH functional groups, when combined with metals, has a magnetic moment ranging from 4.56 to 4.90 µB. These findings suggest that the interaction between C19H11S and metals leads to an augmentation in magnetic moment. The C19H11S+Fe complex had the maximum change in magnetic moment, measuring 0.36 µB, whereas the C19H11S+Sb complex showed the smallest change in magnetic moment, at 0.01 µB.

The CQD (C31H16O3P) functionalized with H2PO3 and metals display a magnetic moment that varies between 4.73 and 4.94 µB. These findings indicate that the interaction between metals and C31H16O3P leads to an augmentation in magnetic moment. The C31H16O3P + Fe combination exhibited the maximum change in magnetic moment, of about 0.21 µB. In contrast, the change in magnetic moment for C31H16O3P + Sb was the lowest, at 0.005 µB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime of functionalized CQDs in the water phase
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of quenchers (metals) on the lifetime of CQDs is well reported. We have calculated the lifetime of functionalized CQDs in the water phase prior to and following metal adsorption [50]. The range of lifetime obtained demonstrates that fluorescence is found in the functionalized CQDs. Table S4 giving details on a lifetime of functionalized CQDs with metals has been provided in SI. The functionalized CQDs have a lifetime ranging from 0.48 to 3.82 nanoseconds (ns). The OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15), has the highest lifetime of 3.82 ns, while the NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) has the lowest lifetime of 0.48 ns. The range of lifetime obtained indicates fluorescence in all the systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime of functionalized CQDs with toxic metals in the water phase

ce:section.ce:para: The COOH-functionalized CQD (C32H15O2) displays fluorescence upon the adsorption of metals, with a lifetime ranging from 1.55 to 9.48 ns. A similar finding was reported on COOH-functionalized carbon dots during the detection of Fe3+ ions [30]. The C32H15O2+Cr (9.48 ns) complex has exhibited the longest lifetime. The C32H15O2+Sb (1.55 ns) complex exhibits the shortest lifetime.

The OH-functionalized CQD (C31O15H15) exhibits fluorescence with a lifetime ranging from 2.82 to 12.31 ns when interacting with metals. A similar finding was reported on OH-functionalized carbon dots during the detection of Fe3+ ions [30]. The C31O15H15+Cr (12.31 ns) has the longest lifetime, whereas the complex C31O15H15+Sb (2.82 ns) has the shortest lifetime.

The NH2-functionalized CQD (C31H16N) complexed with metals displays fluorescence with a lifetime ranging from 0.51 to 31.52 ns. Observations have revealed that C31H16N + Fe (31.52 ns) has the highest lifetime. The shortest lifetime was obtained for the complex C31H16N + Sb (0.51 ns).

The lifetime range of SH-functionalized CQD (C19H11S) with metals is in the range 1.40–6.92 ns. The results we obtained for Fe and Hg are consistent with the findings reported on L-cysteine functionalized carbon dot during the detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions [34]. The complex, C19H11S+Fe has the highest lifetime of 6.92 ns, while the complex C19H11S+Cd compound has the shortest lifetime of 1.40 ns.

The H2PO3-functionalized CQD (C31H16O3P) with metals exhibits a lifetime that varies between 0.85 and 4.00 ns. The system C31H16O3P + Fe exhibited the longest lifetime of 4.00 ns, whereas the system C31H16O3P + Cd had the shortest lifetime of 0.85 ns.

We have designed CQDs with five different functional groups (COOH, OH, NH2, SH, and H2PO3) and examined their adsorption ability towards hazardous metals like Fe, Cr, Se, Hg, Sb, and Cd present in drinking water using quantum chemical DFT calculations. The modeled functionalized CQDs are structurally stable. Energy gap analysis has revealed that NH2-functionalized CQDs are more stable than all other functionalized CQDs. All toxic metals in the gas and water phases get adsorbed in the range of −0.01 to −4.02 eV and −0.12 to −4.33 eV, respectively. Among all functionalized CQDs, COOH- and NH2-functionalized CQDs show higher adsorption affinity towards toxic metals. Also, most of the functionalized CQDs show higher adsorption with Cr followed by Fe, Se, Sb, Cd, and Hg. The absorbance peaks of all functionalized CQDs exhibit a redshift as the size of the functionalized CQDs increases, as shown by the UV-Vis absorption spectra. The structural integrity of the functionalized CQDs that are modeled has been verified by Raman spectra. Further, the metal complexation with the functionalized CQDs in the water phase has been validated through absorption peak intensity modulation in the UV-Vis absorption spectra. It is found that the highest intensity reduction percentage has been observed after adsorption of Fe with COOH-functionalized C32H15O2 (69.91 %), Se with OH-functionalized CQD C31O15H15 (44.56 %), Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD C31H16N (98.42 %), Cr with SH-functionalized CQD C19H11S (39.16 %), Fe with H2PO3-functionalized CQD C31H16O3P (71.48 %). Furthermore, it is shown that following metal adsorption, NH2-functionalized CQDs exhibit the largest percentage decrease in absorbance in UV-Vis absorption spectra when compared to other functionalized CQDs. The magnetic moment of functionalized CQDs before and after adsorption with metals was studied, and we observed an increase in magnetic moment upon adsorption with metals. Reasonably, a large fluorescence lifetime is obtained for Fe with NH2-functionalized CQD C31H16N (31.52 ns) in functionalized CQDs. To summarize, our in-depth DFT studies have shown that functionalized CQDs can be employed as promising toxic metal adsorbents/detectors in potable water for effective water remediation/quality monitoring purposes. Also, our research may help to design quantum sensors for similar applications.
